summary: Emma came very late, 25 minutes late, and she left early. She was in a rather good mood, but she didn't understand some of the vocabulary, so she asked me to tell some of the words in French. She looked a bit desperate because she didn't understand what was going on, so she was a bit upset about that. When she saw that it was a writing task, she looked very happy not to have to do it because she was leaving at that time. But she did the grammar vocabulary very well, and she only needed a little reminder that after used to, we had to use a verb in -ing.

Emma was the only student who came to class because Alice, Romain and Hélène were absent. The class went really well: she was much more confident, could take her time to answer, and looked very comfortable asking me questions. She is more willing to make efforts and try to get the right answers when I give her more time or do not give her the answers straight away. She was very attentive and polite, like always, and she read the text quite well; her reading was really, really impressive. She still sometimes makes errors when she pronounces some words, but I told her I found her very good today and that I was very happy and proud of her.

Emma could use some of the grammatical structures and remembered a little bit of the previous chapters. I was quite happy with her work, and she was quite serious with it. She was also quite happy and in a focused, happy mood, even though she was a bit playful with Elena.

Emma took part in the activities quite well and could use the target language in the gapped sentences quite well. She was trying to avoid speaking as much as possible, and when I asked her to speak, she did. She was a bit on the defensive side about the writing task, said she would do it if she had time, and she sometimes asks for vocabulary words in French because she lacks them. She also sometimes makes mistakes when she speaks, especially with pronouns, and sometimes forgets tenses or the third-person singular -s.
Helena helped her a lot with the vocabulary. Emma still asks sometimes in French or asks for vocabulary words because she lacks them, so she doesn't know them.
Emma was more comfortable with Romain, and so she did the oral exercises well too. She still makes some mistakes, and she can usually correct them, so that's great. I was really proud of her.

Emma needed help because she couldn't remember what old-fashioned meant. They could do the listening activity quite well and could report what the characters had said quite well, although I needed to tell Emma to mind the 's' at the 3rd person singular. I helped Emma with her My Talent task. She couldn't spell 'teen' but I asked Romain to help her and he did.

Emma was calm and attentive and did the work she was asked as well as she could. She could read the chapter quite well, without pronunciation mistakes, but she was not totally fluent. She could ask for words she did not understand, though she asked them in French to Ylena. She could summarize the chapters using the target language written on the board rather well, make questions from the target language, answer using it too, and understand a song and answer questions about it. She could form a question and an answer using "would rather…than…" and "prefer… to…". She seemed a bit uneasy with me, but it got better by the end. She could not really use vocabulary words and make sentences with them that showed understanding of the words.

They could all express themselves rather well when talking about food groups, dishes and meals. They could use the structures given by the teacher very well, and they worked on an argumentative text quite well. When we worked collaboratively, Emma participated, but when it was individual, she asked to go to the loo and stayed there a bit. Emma and Romain needed a bit of help for inspiration, and Emma sometimes needed a bit of help with pronunciation. She knew how to use the connector but had to be reminded of its purpose.

Emma made some efforts to talk a bit more and expand her answers, being more confident. Romain and Emma had forgotten how to use connectors such as although and despite. Emma still makes some pronunciation mistakes and sometimes makes tense mistakes like using "do" for "he/she." The class could do the exercises quite well and understand the new vocabulary very well too, and they all did an effort to express themselves.

The group went well overall: they were all polite, motivated, and interested. They could remember parts of the story quite well, answer using full structures, understand the vocabulary well, and do the games well too. Emma struggled a bit with pronunciation, but could repeat the correct words. She does not always try and says that she does not know, so I need to encourage her to try, which she will sometimes do. She asked for something to Alice in French, and I told her to ask in English, which she did. In the My Talent Body 1 part, she made mistakes mainly about capital letters and punctuation.

The Fairy Tales 2 students were all motivated. They could all do the exercises well enough, make sentences with a comparative, understand and hear the mistakes in the songs, and read very well. Emma was sometimes making a couple of mistakes with the pronunciation.

Emma could use the causative form (get something done) correctly. She could create a problem and give a solution using the target language. She could review the target language by using words from the previous chapters in a sentence. She could turn nouns into adjectives. She seemed less comfortable giving out full answers today. She could find vocabulary words in a text and answer questions about her reading.
